sentences.text: Yes.
Yes.
It was approximately on February the 5th in 1945.
We were evacuated from the camp, which was in Wolfsburg.
Not the whole camp.
There were a lot of sick people and a lot of-- somehow, for some reason, didn't go, but then approximately were 3,000.
I don't think that all the 3,000.
Several hundred remained there.
And we  are starting to march.
They took us approximately three days on a death march, we call it.
Death march.
It was very, very bad weather.
Many people on the way couldn't go.
They were shot to death.
I don't know what happened to them.
But there was also a carriage, or two carriages, going behind us to collecting these dead people who couldn't go anymore or who were shot.
They were shot-- I have seen by myself-- by SS-- by young SS people.
I don't know who these guards were who were leading us, because they were mostly younger, not older men anymore.
And so we went through all kinds villages, marching and marching for about--
On foot.
On foot, about two or three days.
More.
We start to march Monday.
We marched until Friday.
During the-- the [INAUDIBLE], many were killed for some reason.
I don't know why.
There's no numbers I can tell you.
We arrived to a small, little town, and they pushed us in-- in a barn.
There was snow.
And this barn could have-- probably 500 could have been fit in.
Impossible, unbelievable.
They started to push on and in and in and in.
And so they pushed in until everyone was in-- was in there and locked.
People couldn't even stand now.
Can you imagine, if someone-- people were weak very much-- if someone, somehow bent down, he was finished, he was not more alive.
So as time went by, what happened to me, I somehow [INAUDIBLE], and there was some hook, and I was holding myself near a hook on a Friday night, as I can remember this.
Was in the March of-- in-- in February 1945.
So I survived this night.
And  as the night went-- passed by more and more, it became more and more roomier, because many people fell-- Just fell.
--On the floor, and natural died.
So I still had the strength.
Now, I-- in the morning, they let us out again, the German SS.
And I see they started to make big-- a big hall.
So all those who were dead, they put them in that hall.
As a matter of fact, I have seen three or four of my friends whom I remember.
One was a dentist.
I saw him [? dead ?] there.
And so we stay there.
I don't exactly how long, maybe for another week or so.
And horrible to describe was inside.
They slaughtered a cow or something like this, and they give something to eat, but not too much.
This was near a town where there was Germans.
They were speaking German.
I don't know exactly the town.
At one point, they went and brought us near a station.
Probably that station was there, not far.
I don't know the name, because I never looked up, and I don't remember [INAUDIBLE].
And whoever was alive, they put in vans.
And they gave, for each one, a bread to divide at five, whoever he wants.
So we were in open vans.
We had open vans, not cattle.
We had open vans.
And so they started-- the train started to go, day and night.
People were very tired.
People started to die there in the trains, near us.
And people started-- some were so thirsty, it's impossible.
We stopped at station.
It was amazing.
Germans were there.
Women were there.
Many other people.
No one gave a drink of water.
People were yelling, all kind, no one there to come there.
We were waiting that rain will come to have our tongue at least something to wet down.
I, myself, also experienced my-- when I, myself, had drunk my own urine.
Impossible, but it's true.
Many people were drinking their urine.
And the bread, which-- which I had somehow by pieces, give for one for another.
And so in this train, we went.
An open train.
I don't know for how many days, but I remember we went to a station.
Leipzig was bombarded, then we went to a station Halle, until we came to Bergen-Belsen.
Now, I say now Bergen-Belsen.
In that time, I didn't know Bergen-Belsen exist.
That for sure, we came there was on a Friday night.
Two friends of mine, were two brothers, by the name of Berger.
They were from Beregsaz.
Young boys, they were looking like Muselmann.
On the way as we were going, they fell down on the floor and-- I mean, on the street.
They were marching us on the street somewhere.
It was snowing a little bit.
And so several peoples on the street, I don't know what happened to them.
They died as going from the train to this camp.
We came in this camp.
I never heard about Bergen-Belsen.
Don't know about Bergen-Belsen.
Now I know that it's Bergen-Belsen.
And inside, as we come in, it was snowing.
It was a snow, a little snow.
I see you cannot go no place.
They let us loose already inside, but they see it was some sort of a camp.
People are lying one after another, death, death.
They never even stop at a [? death ?] person.
So they put us in-- not us, certain people, whoever.
I went in in block 5.
This I remember for sure.
Block 5 in Bergen-Belsen.
This block 5 was near a place where the rutabagas were.
You know these-- which they make soup of it sometimes.
You know, it's-- it's-- it's a vegetable.
Rutabagas.
Rutabagas.
Rutabaga, yeah.
Big, a big-- but it was fenced up with a thing.
We slept there, three in a place.
Three, four, no place where-- what odor.
No toilet, no facilities, until the morning.
Morning, woke up, and I see there's people all around.
It's not-- But over there I've seen already, there are not only Jews, there are some other nationalities.
I don't know whether they were Jews, but they are from France, people.
There are people from other countries.
I didn't ask them whether we are Jew or not Jew.
Didn't ask.
And this is Bergen-Belsen.
At one point, they asked-- they didn't get food.
Very little.
A little bread, maybe a little soup.
In this block 5 where I lived was three stages of beds, like in all these camps, but [RUSSIAN] they called it Russian.
And it was impossible there.
You had to climb up, and people were-- so people on the floor lay down.
There was no order.
There was nothing.
Then it was completely without any order.
There was no blockalteste, no stubenalteste.
There was some complete disorder, whatever.
Inside, in the room, in the middle, they had a very big dish as a toilet, so people-- whoever want to in that.
In the morning, I've seen some people went to work.
They came out and ask if who wants to go to work.
I understand Bergen-Belsen wasn't a camp to work.
And who is going to work will get a piece of bread or will get, in the evening, some soup.
At one point, I also-- I'm going to work.
They took me to a place, the little-- it wasn't a farm, but they had grass.
They had to take out the grass.
We had about 100 people or over 100.
There came a German SS man, what I've seen with my eye, and started to hit a young Jew there.
Wanted to kill him, but they didn't want to kill him, just plainly shoot him.
He wanted-- he made up his mind he is going to kill him for [? a reason. ?] He didn't kill him completely, but the man, as we finished work, couldn't go home.
He wasn't on his feet.
He ordered four people should take him on his shoulders.
He was close to death.
The rest were also to death-- Muselmann.
Were-- and we came back to camp on the plantation.
All those who went to work will get some piece of bread.
They lined up those who weren't in for bread, and I thought also I was in that line, but people who didn't go to work also mixed in there, because with the yard, you couldn't find out.
By the time I came there, was no bread, was no food.
One day, they tried it with three people.
I still had a little strength there myself.
We are going here-- the hunger was terrible.
It's impossible.
We were without food for days already.
We are going to make something.
When we bring the kettle, you usually bring a kettle with soup.
We are going to come there, three people, and two will do the following.
One will grab the kettle, and the other one will escape, because they should run after them, they should notice who.
The run will escape, run away.
They should run after him that he was the one.
In the meantime, one will grab and put in his dish.
And I was among the three.
So it happened that I was lucky enough that I was standing near the-- near the thing, and they attacked.
Two people attacked the other, and one started to run away.
So they went and the people who   saw them started to hit him.
I think they killed him there.
And I, in the meantime, I did get some-- a little of that food in my dish.
I grabbed at my dish.
And the other guy also escaped.
They never knew who.
And they were running after the man who was supposed to escape who didn't do nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bergen-Belsen, the worst part was of its lice.
Lice was all over, but here was something unbelievable.
I, myself, became very sick.
I didn't know what it means.
I didn't know what sick is.
I start to hurt, my head, my-- I think I was already for about two or three weeks.
And I just couldn't get up from my [? bed. ?] People who did get up, in the sun, was some people, they're sitting in the block there, but that's all.
They were trying somehow through this fence to take a rutabaga, but they couldn't reach it.
Somehow with sticks and somehow were beaten if they saw them.
But they were grabbing it.
Now, I was lucky in-- in-- in Bergen-Belsen.
I brought about six cigarettes when I arrived.
So in the beginning, in the first days, someone had a little honey.
I don't know from where.
[INAUDIBLE].
Whoever was there from France and asked if someone had cigarettes.
Yes, he'll give me a piece of honey for a very-- Probably was from the Red Cross did get somewhere.
He was from-- from France.
And so-- and another gave me a piece of bread.
I gave him a cigarette.
So with this six cigarettes which I had, somehow I was able to survive a little time.
No one knew  I had it in my pocket.
These cigarettes were already mushed up and all kind.
So it came to the point where, I have seen with my own eyes, when I walked-- walked out there, there was cannibalism.
But they were-- I don't know whether Jews did it or non-Jews, but we are already at Bergen-Belsen a mixed up of-- of people.
Were talking in French.
Were talking in Russian.
Greeks were there.
It's a mix.
A lot of Jews, but it's a mixture.
I've seen several of my friends die there, who were sitting on the floor.
They couldn't get up anymore.
It took them under, the dead.
It's-- about Bergen-Belsen, I can talk hours and hours.
But I'll come to the final point.
At one point, those who were very sick, they didn't know.
Block elders, or whoever were in charge-- not Germans, those were in charge, maybe they were appointed-- came.
And for some people, they put here on the thigh here, they give a number.
What this number meant, I don't [? know. ?] With a pencil, with a crayon pencil, they give a number.
What this meant, I don't know.
Whoever couldn't go out were laid in such a manner.
Well, there were several.
No one wanted to stay.
Everyone wanted to go out in the sun.
There was no work, but they didn't have the strength.
I was laying there also, and I figured out, there must be a reason.
Maybe they take them to someplace where it is more safer there, so  I didn't realize.
But then I see that all those people, next day, the following day, had been taken by not guards, but inmates, also who had a wagon, you know, like a carriage of two rails or four.
I don't remember.
They put on the dead who were dead.
And so I was laying there among with two people, and the third thing, I couldn't move.
But one was a very redhead, a young fellow, but he was unconscious completely.
Whether he was Jewish or not Jewish.
Near me-- nearby was a person who probably at that night died.
We were three there.
So they came to give a piece of-- three pieces of bread.
They had the bread for the-- daily, when they distributed.
They asked me how many.
Three.
So I took the three pieces, but I had to give for the other guy, one piece.
One piece I took a small little piece of bread.
I ate it up, and I was very hungry.
The other guy who was sitting near me couldn't eat anymore.
And the red guy-- maybe he was Russian, because there were some Russians also who for some reason came.
I started to grab from him the bread, but I didn't have strength.
And he was holding onto his bread.
I figured, well, I need very badly this little piece of bread.
I started to choke him a little bit.
I didn't have either strength.
Somehow he let out from his hand the bread, and I took his bread away.
I have seen also during my time there, father by the name-- they were from Wolowa.
Forgot their name.
It doesn't comes to mind.
Father and son.
One killed the other one.
I think the father killed the son.
Piece of bread.
It was by the name Weingarten.
Pincus was his son, a good friend of mine from Beregsaz.
I still was-- I had strength before I was sick.
He asked me-- he was sitting on the floor, it was funny-- he asked me, I should give him the hand to lift him up, because otherwise he stays here, he would not be able to get up.
I gave him my hand, I want to lift up, but I fell on him, and I was glad that I could get up myself and then my friend.
Finally was the following that I did get a number too.
I didn't realize exactly, I didn't know.
Because I still wasn't so-- I still was conscious, and I still could talk, but horrible typhoid I had and lice.
It-- it is almost indescribable.
So they came and gave me-- just put on this number on me.
Put on the number.
I couldn't move.
I couldn't do anything.
Next day, in the morning-- usually used to come in the morning-- and I see in the wagon, they take out a few dead from here.
And I didn't know where they put them, how they put them, where.
It was probably on a-- on a-- on a larger piece.
Not far was also where they put on a-- on a pile dead.
Those who were laying on the floor were laying, but from the room they wanted take out.
So and he drags me down from-- from that and starts to-- two of them-- starts to drag me also to this.
On the wagon were laying already two dead people.
One was still alive.
He was a Hungarian.
He was telling, but I'm-- in Hungarian-- [HUNGARIAN], he says, I still am alive.
He was also, but he couldn't move.
He puts me on also on that wagon, like among this, I know for sure brings me there to the-- to that pile.

As he goes with his wagon, I somehow-- one foot of mine was somehow wasn't-- didn't fit in, and my foot somehow was over leaning-- I didn't have the strength-- and somehow from that wagon, I slipped, and not voluntarily, and I fell on the floor, and he continued to go further.
He didn't pay attention.
So I was also among those dead there, so probably didn't pay attention to come back and take me again.
At that point, a man--
No, I-- no, not involuntarily slipped out.
I was-- I was somehow-- there was this small wagon, and I was laying.
He didn't put me on the right way.
He didn't put me on the right way.
He didn't fit me in that I should be among these people.
And so I slipped out, and a man by the name of Isidore Spiegel, I believe is right-- and came  and saw me.
Sorry, you are still here.
He didn't see me for a while.
He was from my town.
I couldn't answer.
He knew what's going on.
He [INAUDIBLE]-- he wasn't sick.
He had made some little fire there and had some boiling water.
Most of the people, they get-- he brought me a little water from the boiling water.
I was laying down among.
And he says to me, he started to drag me, and dragged me away from there, and put me also in a corner there, and he says, you stay here, and don't go, please.
I'll bring you a little bit more water.
Bring me a little bit more water.
And so this was about two days before liberation.
I went back somehow.
He put me back to my bed where I was.
Wasn't-- already wasn't too many there in the beds.
They are taking out-- ever-- they were bringing in new and new people.
In Bergen-Belsen, many other things I've seen also.
Already cannibalism, I've seen that on several occasions.
I cannot describe.
I was looking, but I run away from it also, despite [? the war. ?]
I don't know who were these people.
It's impossible.
They were looking for liver, something like this.
I don't know why.
There was a fire there.
And so it came on April 15, 1945.
Laying there, I didn't know what's happening, nothing.
I've seen that the Germans went away, where there were too many, they started to talk.
Some French people came in.
I didn't pay attention.
It was an afternoon on a Sunday.
April the 15th, maybe around 3 o'clock in there.
Well, from what I understand, the English came in there.
England, they liberated Bergen-Belsen.
I, at that time, didn't know.
I didn't know even I am in Bergen-Belsen, despite I was there.
I know a bit of Belgian they were talking, but [INAUDIBLE] And so they liberated.
But here comes the very big thing.
They gave me, in the evening, a can of some meat.
I ate it up completely.
I was sick, almost died.
The worst part of it, I was laying there for two more weeks with the lice.
No-- well, they came every day.
They brought something.
They didn't give the same food.
Two weeks after.
Well, first they took out the women, the girls, the very sick.
I was also.
The men, they didn't take.
It take two weeks.
But at one point, they came, and they lined us up.
People are-- somewhere they were standing.
And they had a machine, a blower, and they blow onto your body some powder, which the lice went away right away.
But to have lice that it is indescribable.
And so I was liberated after two weeks.
They brought me to [NON-ENGLISH] I was in [NON-ENGLISH] in a hospital for a few days until I recuperated.
Then I found out I was in typhoid.
Didn't know what it is.
But hundreds and hundreds died after the liberation because of the food or because they couldn't-- they couldn't survive anymore.
But the two weeks which I was after-- almost two weeks after liberation.

And from [NON-ENGLISH] I was a few-- I do not remember, a few weeks from [NON-ENGLISH], they announced whoever belongs to Czechoslovakia wants to go back to his hometown, so he could go to his hometown.
Let me finish with one thing small.
After going through all these things, which I didn't describe in details, only highlights, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of my time, I have one desire.
And if I can fulfill these three things, I don't care what's going to happen to my life.
Number one, if I'll ever in life be able to have a piece of bread, that's something.
Only those who are hungry can understand.
Thank God to have a piece of bread.
Secondly, I must take revenge.
Unfortunately.
I was liberated.
I was in [NON-ENGLISH].
I have seen small children.
Am I going to kill them?
For what?
What they did to me?
Am I going to shoot?
Am I going to kill a German on the street because, who knows, his father?
So I couldn't do.
But I'm going to tell the world.
So let me finish with this one here.
I'm so glad and happy about this that I could tell this to the world.
And maybe-- I'm 80 years-old, maybe you're a little bit younger.
Maybe one day, you'll say to yourself and your children, yes, we were privileged and lucky to hear these stories from a survivor who survived this, not from-- we read from books.
And from what I know, from what I think, from now, and 5, and 8, and 10 years, you  are not going to have any more survivors.
So we are the last one.

And what you are doing, you are doing to mankind a great, a great, a great-- how should I tell you-- you contribute something to this, not that shouldn't we forget, that something like this existed even.
Many people don't want to believe it.
I, myself, I went through only-- through highlights.
Shall I be bitter now?
I'm getting some reparation.
What does it mean, this reparation?
Can they pay for my family, for my father, for my sister, for my immediate family?
And you know what a family I had.
So I don't know how the world will look, but one thing let me tell you.
Why'd all this what happen?
Who were these perpetrators?
These perpetrators were just plain people like you and you, who went to church on Sunday and had children also.
But in a sense, they were murderers.
Were they all murderers?
Yes.
But how about those who were silent, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who were not even lookers, who know about it and were silent?
They owe also a lot to their conscience.
Because seeing if someone is being killed and being quiet is not the proper way.
We are humans, all.
For five years, the world has seen, the pope has seen-- not knew about it.
Churchill knew about it, so did Roosevelt knew it.
People were asking to bombard Auschwitz when it was apparent also.
We also allowed our-- to those who were silent-- and believe it or not, I blame a lot of Jews in the United States.
Oh, at that time, maybe they didn't have influence.
Why wasn't at that time America [INAUDIBLE]?
Not that time was America [INAUDIBLE] if its philosophy.
If 5,000 Jews at that time went to Washington and lay down near the White House and not going away until something.
I don't say they would have saved all the Jews.
I don't say that.
But the world should know.
I can give you a lot of examples.
Had been Hitler and his been warned, there will be consequences, that you'll be judged, and you will be responsible, probably they would have tried, sort of afraid what to do.
So to blame this entire world, who were quiet, who were silent, who didn't say a word.
Will it repeat as itself?
I don't know.
I don't believe it will repeat in this form, that scientists will come in the next century, and will invent a machine that how to kill people like in Auschwitz they invented.
Maybe in a different form.
Maybe on a smaller scale.
Maybe it will not be publicized.
So let's learn a lesson from this.
My conclusion is the following.
Anyone who didn't experience it on his own cannot understand it and cannot have a feeling for it.
Yes, it happened.
We know.
We pity.
We are with them.
But it didn't happen to me.
I wasn't there for some reason or another.
I thank God I was liberated, and to live someplace, found my wife, who comes from the same area.
We have children.
We have grandchildren.
And certainly, we are very successful.
I'm very thankful for America, because I came here with $1.
With no trade, with nothing.
I worked hard with my life, established a family, didn't look for giveaways.
Knew what my father taught me, that I have to earn.
And maybe I was luckier than someone else, maybe in my trade or whatever it is.
I have more than someone, but not more.
Doesn't make a difference.
There's no poor people here.
Poverty was in our town, whoever would remember it.
Here is no poverty.
I don't believe in that poverty.
And again, again, I must tell you my conclusion.
I'm very, very grateful to you, both of you, to the filmmaker and to interviewer.
I cherish very much your knowledge.
Because really, I found a person, Miss Baker, in you, that you are so familiar, not only in my area, but I can understand you're familiar-- the whole Holocaust.
And you are listening with patience, and I wish I would have more [LAUGHS] time, maybe I can tell you more.
And thank you once again from my deep of my heart.
And any time you have some questions, or you want to know more about something, whatever I can, very gladly and with great pleasure.
Once again, thank you very much.
There are some pictures.
